summary: Gruber rounds up reviews of the 10.5-inch iPad Pro, highlighting the ProMotion 120Hz display as the standout feature that reviewers universally praise. He expresses hope that ProMotion will come to the iPhone since it requires no additional sensors, unlike True Tone. He pushes back against reviewers who frame the iPad Pro as failing to replace laptops, arguing that Tim Cook's quote about iPad replacing PCs was deliberately truncated to seem more absolute than intended. Gruber defends Apple's strategy of keeping iPad and Mac separate rather than merging them Surface-style, noting that preferring a laptop is different from declaring laptops objectively better. He also addresses the display resolution choice, explaining why Apple went with a slightly larger screen rather than cramming the 12.9-inch pixel count into a smaller form factor.
key_points: ProMotion's 120Hz display is the defining feature of the new iPad Pro, and unlike True Tone, it requires no additional sensors — making iPhone adoption likely
The Verge's complaint that a $650 iPad should be your 'main computer' misunderstands Apple's market positioning — Apple stuff isn't for the budget-conscious
The NYT's Brian Chen truncated Tim Cook's iPad Pro quote to make it sound like a universal PC replacement claim rather than the qualified 'for many, many people' statement Cook actually made
Personal preference for laptops over iPad is not the same as laptops being objectively better — Apple's dual-product strategy acknowledges different user needs
Apple chose a slightly larger screen with the same pixel density rather than higher density with the 12.9-inch resolution, avoiding the readability problems that would cause
money_quotes: Nobody outside Apple cares as much about iPad as he does.
I don't agree with the notion that a $650 computer should be your 'main computer' at all. Apple stuff isn't for the budget-conscious — news at 11.
'I prefer a laptop to an iPad Pro' is very different from 'A laptop is better than an iPad Pro'.
That's the whole point of Apple's strategy of keeping them separate, rather than unifying them Microsoft Surface-style.
There's no reason to doubt that 'many, many people' are concluding they no longer need a Mac or PC.
You can suss out a lot about Apple's priorities from the aspects of a product it leaves alone and the ones it never stops obsessing over.
ProMotion doesn't require additional sensors — only a super-fast GPU and intricate software support in iOS.
key_insight: The iPad Pro's value proposition is misunderstood by reviewers who insist on judging it as a laptop replacement rather than accepting Apple's deliberate strategy of maintaining two distinct computing platforms for different users.
